From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

International figure skating competition

This article is about the figure skating competition. For the tennis tournament, see [WTA Poland Open](/wiki/WTA_Poland_Open "WTA Poland Open").

The **Warsaw Cup** is an annual [figure skating competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU), organized and hosted by the Polish Figure Skating Association ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *Polski Związek Łyżwiarstwa Figurowego*) and held in [Warsaw, Poland](/wiki/Warsaw,_Poland "Warsaw, Poland"). The Warsaw Cup debuted in 2002 as a junior-level competition. A pairs event for senior-level skaters was added in 2010, men's and women's in 2012, and ice dance in 2014. When the ISU launched the [ISU Challenger Series](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series") in 2014, the Warsaw Cup was one of the inaugural competitions. The Warsaw Cup has been a Challenger Series almost every year since. Medals are awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"); and as part of the Challenger Series, skaters earn [ISU World Standing](/wiki/ISU_World_Standings_and_Season%27s_World_Ranking "ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking") points based on their results.

[Alexander Majorov](/wiki/Alexander_Majorov "Alexander Majorov") of Sweden and [Vladimir Samoilov](/wiki/Vladimir_Samoilov_(figure_skater) "Vladimir Samoilov (figure skater)") of Poland are tied for winning the most Warsaw Cup titles in men's singles (with two each), while [Ekaterina Kurakova](/wiki/Ekaterina_Kurakova "Ekaterina Kurakova") of Poland holds the record in women's singles (with three). [Valentina Marchei](/wiki/Valentina_Marchei "Valentina Marchei") and [Ondřej Hotárek](/wiki/Ond%C5%99ej_Hot%C3%A1rek "Ondřej Hotárek") of Italy, and [Anastasiia Metelkina](/wiki/Anastasiia_Metelkina "Anastasiia Metelkina") and [Luka Berulava](/wiki/Luka_Berulava "Luka Berulava") of Georgia, are tied for winning the most titles in pair skating (with two each). [Evgeniia Lopareva](/wiki/Evgeniia_Lopareva "Evgeniia Lopareva") and [Geoffrey Brissaud](/wiki/Geoffrey_Brissaud "Geoffrey Brissaud") of France hold the record in ice dance (with three).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warsaw_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The inaugural edition of the Warsaw Cup was held in 2002 and consisted only of the men's and women's events at the junior level.[[1]](#cite_note-WC2002-1) A junior-level pairs event was added in 2003. The Warsaw Cup was exclusively a junior-level competition until 2010, when a senior-level pairs event was added.[[2]](#cite_note-WC2010-2) Men's and women's events at the senior level were added in 2012.[[3]](#cite_note-WC2012-3)

The [ISU Challenger Series](/wiki/ISU_Challenger_Series "ISU Challenger Series") was introduced in 2014. It is a series of international figure skating competitions sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") and organized by [ISU member nations](/wiki/ISU_Member_Nations "ISU Member Nations"). The objective is to ensure consistent organization and structure within a series of international competitions linked together, providing opportunities for senior-level skaters to compete at the international level and also earn [ISU World Standing](/wiki/ISU_World_Standings_and_Season%27s_World_Ranking "ISU World Standings and Season's World Ranking") points.[[4]](#cite_note-4) When the Warsaw Cup became a Challenger Series event beginning in 2014, that marked the end of its junior-level competitions.[[5]](#cite_note-WC2014-5) Ice dance at the senior level was also added in 2014 to coincide with the competition's new Challenger Series status.[[5]](#cite_note-WC2014-5) The Warsaw Cup was a Challenger Series event from 2014 to 2017. The Warsaw Cup was held in 2018, but not as part of the Challenger Series, and junior-level competitions in the men's and women's events, and ice dance, made a one-time reappearance.[[6]](#cite_note-WC2018-6) It returned to the Challenger Series in 2019.[[7]](#cite_note-WC2019-7) No competition was held in 2020 due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports#Figure_skating "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports").[[8]](#cite_note-WC2020-8) The competition returned in 2021 and has been a Challenger Series event ever since.

*From left to right:* [Alexander Majorov](/wiki/Alexander_Majorov "Alexander Majorov") of Sweden has won two Warsaw Cup titles in [men's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"); [Ekaterina Kurakova](/wiki/Ekaterina_Kurakova "Ekaterina Kurakova") of Poland has won three Warsaw Cup titles in [women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"); [Valentina Marchei](/wiki/Valentina_Marchei "Valentina Marchei") and [Ondřej Hotárek](/wiki/Ond%C5%99ej_Hot%C3%A1rek "Ondřej Hotárek") of Italy, and [Anastasiia Metelkina](/wiki/Anastasiia_Metelkina "Anastasiia Metelkina") and [Luka Berulava](/wiki/Luka_Berulava "Luka Berulava") of Georgia, have each won two Warsaw Cup titles in [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"); and [Evgeniia Lopareva](/wiki/Evgeniia_Lopareva "Evgeniia Lopareva") and [Geoffrey Brissaud](/wiki/Geoffrey_Brissaud "Geoffrey Brissaud") of France have won two Warsaw Cup titles in [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance").
